texts.orig: Nested Instrumental Variables Design: Switcher Average Treatment Effect, Identification, Efficient Estimation and Generalizability

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract : Instrumental variables (IV) are a commonly used tool to estimate causal effects from nonrandomized data. An archetype of an IV is a randomized trial with non-compliance where the randomized treatment assignment serves as an IV for the non-ignorable treatment received. Under a monotonicity assumption, a valid IV non-parametrically identifies the average treatment effect among a non-identified, latent complier subgroup, whose generalizability is often under debate. In many studies, there could exist multiple versions of an IV, for instance, different nudges to take the same treatment in different study sites in a multicentre clinical trial. These different versions of an IV may result in different compliance rates and offer a unique opportunity to study IV estimates' generalizability. In this article, we introduce a novel nested IV assumption and study identification of the average treatment effect among two latent subgroups: always-compliers and switchers, who are defined based on the joint potential treatment received under two versions of a binary IV. We derive the efficient influence function for the SWitcher Average Treatment Effect (SWATE) under a non-parametric model and propose efficient estimators. We then propose formal statistical tests of the generalizability of IV estimates under the nested IV framework. We apply the proposed method to the Prostate, Lung, Colorectal and Ovarian (PLCO) Cancer Screening Trial and study the causal effect of colorectal cancer screening and its generalizability. Keywords : Generalizability; Instrumental variable; Noncompliance; Principal stratification; Semipara-

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.1 Instrumental variable (IV); complier average treatment effect; generalizability
texts.text: 1.1 Instrumental variable (IV); complier average treatment effect; generalizability

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Scientific inquiries often involve investigating cause-and-effect relationships. Randomization is a powerful tool for drawing valid causal inference. Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) are widely regarded as the gold standard for establishing causality. However, drawing a valid causal conclusion from non-randomized studies is often challenging due to many factors, unmeasured confounding being a primary concern. To address the potential bias introduced by unmeasured confounders, researchers often turn to quasi-experimental methods (Cook et al., 1979), with instrumental variable (IV) methods being among the most widely used approaches (Angrist et al., 1996; Hern´ an and Robins, 2006; Baiocchi et al., 2014). A variable is a valid IV if it is correlated with the treatment, does not have a direct effect on the outcome under investigation and is independent of unmeasured treatment-outcome confounders (see, e.g., Baiocchi et al., 2014). Econometricians popularized IV methods under the framework of linear structural equations with omitted variables (see, e.g., Goldberger, 1972). Angrist et al. (1996) first formalized an IV under the potential outcomes (PO) framework (Neyman, 1923; Rubin, 1974), defined the complier average treatment effect (CATE) as a key estimand and studied its non-parametric identification under a monotonicity assumption which states that the level of a person's treatment received cannot decrease as the level of IV increases. The concept of identifying and estimating causal effects among a latent subpopulation defined by potential outcomes under both treatment assignments (referred to as 'cross-world' potential outcomes) was referred to as principal stratification and later generalized to other contexts, including surrogate endpoints, truncation by death, among others (Frangakis and Rubin, 2002; Zhang and Rubin, 2003; Gilbert and Hudgens, 2008).

Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) are widely regarded as the gold standard for establishing causality. However, drawing a valid causal conclusion from non-randomized studies is often challenging due to many factors, unmeasured confounding being a primary concern. To address the potential bias introduced by unmeasured confounders, researchers often turn to quasi-experimental methods (Cook et al., 1979), with instrumental variable (IV) methods being among the most widely used approaches (Angrist et al., 1996; Hern´ an and Robins, 2006; Baiocchi et al., 2014). A variable is a valid IV if it is correlated with the treatment, does not have a direct effect on the outcome under investigation and is independent of unmeasured treatment-outcome confounders (see, e.g., Baiocchi et al., 2014). Econometricians popularized IV methods under the framework of linear structural equations with omitted variables (see, e.g., Goldberger, 1972). Angrist et al. (1996) first formalized an IV under the potential outcomes (PO) framework (Neyman, 1923; Rubin, 1974), defined the complier average treatment effect (CATE) as a key estimand and studied its non-parametric identification under a monotonicity assumption which states that the level of a person's treatment received cannot decrease as the level of IV increases. The concept of identifying and estimating causal effects among a latent subpopulation defined by potential outcomes under both treatment assignments (referred to as 'cross-world' potential outcomes) was referred to as principal stratification and later generalized to other contexts, including surrogate endpoints, truncation by death, among others (Frangakis and Rubin, 2002; Zhang and Rubin, 2003; Gilbert and Hudgens, 2008).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A key critique towards the CATE framework, and principal stratification in general, is that the CATE only concerns the causal effect of a latent subgroup and cannot be generalized to the entire population without further assumptions; see, e.g., the extensive discussion in Deaton (2009), Heckman and Urzua (2010), Joffe (2011), among others. One strand of work seeks to illuminate the relationship between the CATE and the average treatment effect (ATE) or the av-
texts.text: A key critique towards the CATE framework, and principal stratification in general, is that the CATE only concerns the causal effect of a latent subgroup and cannot be generalized to the entire population without further assumptions; see, e.g., the extensive discussion in Deaton (2009), Heckman and Urzua (2010), Joffe (2011), among others. One strand of work seeks to illuminate the relationship between the CATE and the average treatment effect (ATE) or the av-

texts.label: text
texts.orig: erage treatment effect on the treated (ATT) by proposing suitable identification assumptions that point identify the ATE with a valid IV. These assumptions typically impose some homogeneity or no-interaction-type restrictions on the data generating processes (Swanson et al., 2018; Levis et al., 2024). For instance, with a binary IV and a binary treatment, Robins (1994) proposed the no-current-treatment-interaction assumption, which states that there is no additive interaction between the IV and treatment in the model for conditional average treatment effect. An alternative assumption is the homogeneous selection bias assumption proposed by Tchetgen Tchetgen and Vansteelandt (2013), which posits that the selection bias function is independent of the value of the IV on an additive scale and identifies the ATT using a valid IV. More recently, Wang and Tchetgen Tchetgen (2018) showed that if there is no additive unmeasured confounder-IV interaction in the model for treatment or additive unmeasured confounder-treatment interaction in the model for potential outcomes, then the ATE can be identified by an IV. These assumptions are typically not empirically verifiable and are scale-specific (i.e., only suitable for an additive or multiplicative scale). Under the principal stratification framework, a similar assumption called principal ignorability, which states that there is no difference in the average potential outcomes across principal strata conditional on baseline covariates, is needed for generalizability (Jo and Stuart, 2009). Unfortunately, the principal ignorability assumption is also not empirically verifiable.

These assumptions are typically not empirically verifiable and are scale-specific (i.e., only suitable for an additive or multiplicative scale). Under the principal stratification framework, a similar assumption called principal ignorability, which states that there is no difference in the average potential outcomes across principal strata conditional on baseline covariates, is needed for generalizability (Jo and Stuart, 2009). Unfortunately, the principal ignorability assumption is also not empirically verifiable.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.2 A clinical trial with two versions of an IV; nested IV
texts.text: 1.2 A clinical trial with two versions of an IV; nested IV

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The archetype of an IV is an RCT with non-compliance, where the treatment assignment serves as a randomized encouragement to receive the treatment, thereby acting as an IV for the actual treatment uptake. The Prostate, Lung, Colorectal and Ovarian (PLCO) Cancer Screening Trial is a multiphasic RCT conducted from 1993 to 2011 at 10 US institutions. A total of 154,897 participants, aged 55 to 74 years, were enrolled and randomized to either the intervention or control arm. Participants in the intervention arm were offered cancer screening exams, while those in the control arm were advised to receive their usual medical care.
texts.text: The archetype of an IV is an RCT with non-compliance, where the treatment assignment serves as a randomized encouragement to receive the treatment, thereby acting as an IV for the actual treatment uptake. The Prostate, Lung, Colorectal and Ovarian (PLCO) Cancer Screening Trial is a multiphasic RCT conducted from 1993 to 2011 at 10 US institutions. A total of 154,897 participants, aged 55 to 74 years, were enrolled and randomized to either the intervention or control arm. Participants in the intervention arm were offered cancer screening exams, while those in the control arm were advised to receive their usual medical care.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two methods of consent were used in this trial. Seven centres employed a single consent process, while the remaining three initially adopted a dual consent process. In centres using the single consent process, enrollment and randomization were consented to simultaneously. In centres using the dual consent process, participants were not informed of the study design, including the
texts.text: Two methods of consent were used in this trial. Seven centres employed a single consent process, while the remaining three initially adopted a dual consent process. In centres using the single consent process, enrollment and randomization were consented to simultaneously. In centres using the dual consent process, participants were not informed of the study design, including the

texts.label: text
texts.orig: type of intervention, during the initial enrollment and first consent. Later, participants randomized to the intervention arm were informed of their treatment assignment and asked to consent again for the administration of the intervention. The results indicated that the overall compliance rate among participants in the single consent process was 91.3%, while the compliance rate among participants in the dual consent process dropped to 71.2%. Due to the high rate of refusal to receive the screening intervention among dual-consent participants, the three screening centres later switched to the single consent process. In a post hoc analysis, Marcus et al. (2014) found that the single versus dual consent process was significantly associated with receiving the cancer screening (odds ratio = 2.2; 95% CI: 2.0 to 2.5), even after adjusting for a comprehensive set of baseline characteristics, including age at randomization, race and ethnicity, gender, education, BMI, comorbidity score, and others.

Later, participants randomized to the intervention arm were informed of their treatment assignment and asked to consent again for the administration of the intervention. The results indicated that the overall compliance rate among participants in the single consent process was 91.3%, while the compliance rate among participants in the dual consent process dropped to 71.2%. Due to the high rate of refusal to receive the screening intervention among dual-consent participants, the three screening centres later switched to the single consent process. In a post hoc analysis, Marcus et al. (2014) found that the single versus dual consent process was significantly associated with receiving the cancer screening (odds ratio = 2.2; 95% CI: 2.0 to 2.5), even after adjusting for a comprehensive set of baseline characteristics, including age at randomization, race and ethnicity, gender, education, BMI, comorbidity score, and others.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The transition from single to dual consent process created a natural experiment that strengthened the encouragement to comply with the treatment assignment. Given Marcus et al.'s (2014) analysis, it is not unreasonable to assume that a complier under the dual consent process would remain a complier had the person enrolled in the study during the single consent stage. We will refer to the assumption that a complier under one binary IV would remain a complier under a second, strengthened IV as a 'nested IV' assumption. In addition to being induced by a sudden policy shift like that in the PLCO trial, multiple versions of an IV, possibly with varying strength, are prevalent in modern-day trials:
texts.text: The transition from single to dual consent process created a natural experiment that strengthened the encouragement to comply with the treatment assignment. Given Marcus et al.'s (2014) analysis, it is not unreasonable to assume that a complier under the dual consent process would remain a complier had the person enrolled in the study during the single consent stage. We will refer to the assumption that a complier under one binary IV would remain a complier under a second, strengthened IV as a 'nested IV' assumption. In addition to being induced by a sudden policy shift like that in the PLCO trial, multiple versions of an IV, possibly with varying strength, are prevalent in modern-day trials:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Multicentre trials. Contemporary multicentre clinical trials often involve a large number of investigative centres in order to recruit a large number of study participants. In multicentre trials, non-compliance was often variable among different investigative centres. The randomization scheme at each centre can be viewed as a different version of an IV.
texts.text: Multicentre trials. Contemporary multicentre clinical trials often involve a large number of investigative centres in order to recruit a large number of study participants. In multicentre trials, non-compliance was often variable among different investigative centres. The randomization scheme at each centre can be viewed as a different version of an IV.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Integrative/Meta-analysis of multiple trials. Compliance rates often vary across different clinical trials investigating the same intervention. For instance, ? , Figure 1 summarizes the study-specific compliance rates in 10 studies of epidural analgesia, with rates ranging from less than 5% to over 50% across these studies. As another example, in different trials testing the same HIV prevention modality (e.g., daily oral prophylaxis) in similar study populations (e.g., heterosexual women in sub-Saharan Africa), compliance rates varied due to a variety of
texts.text: Integrative/Meta-analysis of multiple trials. Compliance rates often vary across different clinical trials investigating the same intervention. For instance, ? , Figure 1 summarizes the study-specific compliance rates in 10 studies of epidural analgesia, with rates ranging from less than 5% to over 50% across these studies. As another example, in different trials testing the same HIV prevention modality (e.g., daily oral prophylaxis) in similar study populations (e.g., heterosexual women in sub-Saharan Africa), compliance rates varied due to a variety of

texts.label: text
texts.orig: factors (Cohen and Baden, 2012; He et al., 2023), including the methods of encouragement to adhere to the prescribed daily pills. The randomization scheme in each study can be viewed as a different version of an IV.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. Implementation trials. Implementation science studies methods to promote the systematic uptake of evidence based interventions ( ? ). An implementation trial that compares different methods to encourage treatment uptake often induces nested IV structures.
texts.text: Implementation trials. Implementation science studies methods to promote the systematic uptake of evidence based interventions ( ? ). An implementation trial that compares different methods to encourage treatment uptake often induces nested IV structures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A nested IV assumption, when specialized to (1) a pooled analysis of a multicentre clinical trial, (2) an integrated analysis of multiple studies, or (3) an implementation trial comparing different encouragement strategies, would state that a participant who was a complier when enrolled in a clinical site or study with a lower compliance rate would remain a complier had the person enrolled in a clinical site or study with a higher compliance rate.
texts.text: A nested IV assumption, when specialized to (1) a pooled analysis of a multicentre clinical trial, (2) an integrated analysis of multiple studies, or (3) an implementation trial comparing different encouragement strategies, would state that a participant who was a complier when enrolled in a clinical site or study with a lower compliance rate would remain a complier had the person enrolled in a clinical site or study with a higher compliance rate.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.3 Implications of a nested IV assumption
texts.text: 1.3 Implications of a nested IV assumption

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While a conventional IV analysis defines latent subpopulations - compliers, always-takers, nevertakers, and defiers - based on potential treatment received under two realizations of one binary IV (Angrist et al., 1996), the nested IV assumption and its associated framework would define subpopulations based on potential treatment received under 2 × 2 = 4 realizations of two binary IVs and impose structures on these latent subpopulations in a way analogous to the monotonicity assumption in the original paper by Angrist et al. (1996). Under the nested IV framework, the average treatment effects of two latent subpopulations, referred to as 'always-compliers' and 'switchers,' respectively, can be non-parametrically identified under suitable conditions. 'Always-compliers' are participants who would comply with the treatment regardless of the specific IV being used; for instance, these are participants who would be compliers regardless of the consent process employed or the clinical site at which they were enrolled. On the other hand, a non-overlapping latent subpopulation called 'switchers' are participants who would be a non-complier (i.e., an always-taker or a never-taker under Angrist et al.'s (1996) monotonicity assumption) under the weaker IV but become a complier under a strengthened IV; in the PLCO trial, these are participants who would not comply under a dual consent process but comply under a single consent process. In addition to identifying and estimating the causal effect among 'always-compliers' and 'switchers,' a com-
texts.text: While a conventional IV analysis defines latent subpopulations - compliers, always-takers, nevertakers, and defiers - based on potential treatment received under two realizations of one binary IV (Angrist et al., 1996), the nested IV assumption and its associated framework would define subpopulations based on potential treatment received under 2 × 2 = 4 realizations of two binary IVs and impose structures on these latent subpopulations in a way analogous to the monotonicity assumption in the original paper by Angrist et al. (1996). Under the nested IV framework, the average treatment effects of two latent subpopulations, referred to as 'always-compliers' and 'switchers,' respectively, can be non-parametrically identified under suitable conditions. 'Always-compliers' are participants who would comply with the treatment regardless of the specific IV being used; for instance, these are participants who would be compliers regardless of the consent process employed or the clinical site at which they were enrolled. On the other hand, a non-overlapping latent subpopulation called 'switchers' are participants who would be a non-complier (i.e., an always-taker or a never-taker under Angrist et al.'s (1996) monotonicity assumption) under the weaker IV but become a complier under a strengthened IV; in the PLCO trial, these are participants who would not comply under a dual consent process but comply under a single consent process. In addition to identifying and estimating the causal effect among 'always-compliers' and 'switchers,' a com-

texts.label: text
texts.orig: parison of the causal effects on these two disjoint subpopulations, possibly conditional on a set of baseline covariates, helps illuminate the generalizability of IV-based estimates and formally test the no-interaction/principal-ignorability-type assumptions. The proposed tests are flexible nonparametric generalizations of classical over-identification tests that incorporate machine learning tools for the estimation of nuisance parameters. Such progress towards establishing the generalizability of IV estimates, to the best of our knowledge, is a novel and useful contribution to the literature.

The proposed tests are flexible nonparametric generalizations of classical over-identification tests that incorporate machine learning tools for the estimation of nuisance parameters. Such progress towards establishing the generalizability of IV estimates, to the best of our knowledge, is a novel and useful contribution to the literature.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The rest of the article is organized as follows. In Section 2, we describe the nested IV design, define the switcher average treatment effect (SWATE) and present main identification results. In Section 4, we propose estimators for SWATE. In Section 5, we propose formal hypothesis testing procedures that examine treatment effect homogeneity among latent subpopulations under the proposed nested IV framework. Numerical experiments are reported in Section 6. We examine the PLCO study using our proposed method and framework in Section 7 and conclude with a discussion in Section 8.
texts.text: The rest of the article is organized as follows. In Section 2, we describe the nested IV design, define the switcher average treatment effect (SWATE) and present main identification results. In Section 4, we propose estimators for SWATE. In Section 5, we propose formal hypothesis testing procedures that examine treatment effect homogeneity among latent subpopulations under the proposed nested IV framework. Numerical experiments are reported in Section 6. We examine the PLCO study using our proposed method and framework in Section 7 and conclude with a discussion in Section 8.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2 Nested IV design
texts.text: 2 Nested IV design

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1 Two versions of treatment assignment
texts.text: 2.1 Two versions of treatment assignment

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Consider a clinical trial centre in the PLCO study, say the Henry Ford Health System, that switched from a dual to a single consent process. The clinical trial comprised two stages. Let G = a denote the dual consent stage, and we use Z ∈ { 0 a , 1 a } to denote a participant being randomized to the control group ( Z = 0 a ) or the intervention group ( Z = 1 a ) in the dual consent stage. In parallel, in the single consent stage, denoted by G = b , we use Z ∈ { 0 b , 1 b } to denote a participant being randomized to the control ( Z = 0 b ) group or the intervention ( Z = 1 b ) group. Importantly, in either stage, participants randomized to the intervention arm may choose not to take the assigned cancer screening procedure, while participants randomized to the control arm may choose to conduct a cancer screening even if this is not part of their usual medical care. In other words, the randomized treatment assignment is only an encouragement, or a nudge, for participants to receive cancer screening, and participants retain the full right not to adhere to the treatment assignment.
texts.text: Consider a clinical trial centre in the PLCO study, say the Henry Ford Health System, that switched from a dual to a single consent process. The clinical trial comprised two stages. Let G = a denote the dual consent stage, and we use Z ∈ { 0 a , 1 a } to denote a participant being randomized to the control group ( Z = 0 a ) or the intervention group ( Z = 1 a ) in the dual consent stage. In parallel, in the single consent stage, denoted by G = b , we use Z ∈ { 0 b , 1 b } to denote a participant being randomized to the control ( Z = 0 b ) group or the intervention ( Z = 1 b ) group. Importantly, in either stage, participants randomized to the intervention arm may choose not to take the assigned cancer screening procedure, while participants randomized to the control arm may choose to conduct a cancer screening even if this is not part of their usual medical care. In other words, the randomized treatment assignment is only an encouragement, or a nudge, for participants to receive cancer screening, and participants retain the full right not to adhere to the treatment assignment.

texts.orig: plications in two trial stages ( G = a or G = b ) and hence resulted in different compliance rates; therefore, we use different notations, 1 a and 1 b (or 0 a and 0 b ), to denote being assigned to the intervention (or control) arm and encouraged to receive cancer screening (or usual care). Our notation acknowledges that there may exist two versions of encouragement and that the Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption (Rubin, 1974, 1980), or SUTVA, may be violated if one single notation (0 or 1) is used instead.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To further fix ideas, consider a second example in the context of HIV prevention implementation trials (Lambdin et al., 2015). To best leverage the multiple effective HIV prevention modalities in the real world settings, scientists are studying the best implementation strategies to encourage people at elevated HIV risk to use the appropriate HIV prevention tools, like daily oral prophylaxis. To this end, trials with different mechanisms to improve drug uptake (e.g., different drug delivery methods) are deployed. In this context, one could use G = a or G = b to denote two separate implementation trials, Z = 1 a or Z = 0 a to denote randomization to a first drug delivery method or a control method, and Z = 1 b or Z = 0 b to denote randomization to a second drug delivery method or a possibly different control method.

To this end, trials with different mechanisms to improve drug uptake (e.g., different drug delivery methods) are deployed. In this context, one could use G = a or G = b to denote two separate implementation trials, Z = 1 a or Z = 0 a to denote randomization to a first drug delivery method or a control method, and Z = 1 b or Z = 0 b to denote randomization to a second drug delivery method or a possibly different control method.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2 Potential outcomes and identification assumptions
texts.text: 2.2 Potential outcomes and identification assumptions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We define potential outcomes and formulate causal estimands under Neyman and Rubin's potential outcomes framework (Neyman, 1923; Rubin, 1974). Suppose we observe n independent and identically distributed samples, D = { O i = ( Z i , X i , D i , Y i ) , i = 1 , ..., n } , from a target population P 0 ∈ P , where P denotes a non-parametric model for the observed data distribution. Throughout the article, we use E P [ · ] to denote taking expectation over some distribution P ∈ P and E P 0 [ · ] taking expectation over the target population. For participant i , X i denotes a set of baseline covariates, Z i a categorical instrumental variable taking value in { 0 a , 0 b , 1 a , 1 b } , D i ∈ { 0 , 1 } a binary variable indicating the treatment a participant actually received, and Y i a real-valued outcome of interest. Let G i denote a stratification variable, that is, define G i = a if Z i ∈ { 0 a , 1 a } , and G i = b if Z i ∈ { 0 b , 1 b } . For the Henry Ford Health System in the PLCO study, G i denotes the trial stage and, consequently, the type of consent process used (e.g., dual or single consent). In the context of an across-center or across-trial integrated analysis, G i would represent the clinical center or the specific trial in which a participant was enrolled.
texts.text: We define potential outcomes and formulate causal estimands under Neyman and Rubin's potential outcomes framework (Neyman, 1923; Rubin, 1974). Suppose we observe n independent and identically distributed samples, D = { O i = ( Z i , X i , D i , Y i ) , i = 1 , ..., n } , from a target population P 0 ∈ P , where P denotes a non-parametric model for the observed data distribution. Throughout the article, we use E P [ · ] to denote taking expectation over some distribution P ∈ P and E P 0 [ · ] taking expectation over the target population. For participant i , X i denotes a set of baseline covariates, Z i a categorical instrumental variable taking value in { 0 a , 0 b , 1 a , 1 b } , D i ∈ { 0 , 1 } a binary variable indicating the treatment a participant actually received, and Y i a real-valued outcome of interest. Let G i denote a stratification variable, that is, define G i = a if Z i ∈ { 0 a , 1 a } , and G i = b if Z i ∈ { 0 b , 1 b } . For the Henry Ford Health System in the PLCO study, G i denotes the trial stage and, consequently, the type of consent process used (e.g., dual or single consent). In the context of an across-center or across-trial integrated analysis, G i would represent the clinical center or the specific trial in which a participant was enrolled.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We will refer to the tuple (0 a , 1 a ) (or (0 b , 1 b )) as an IV dose pair (Sun and W¨ uthrich, 2022). We use Y ( D = d, Z = z ) to denote the potential outcome of Y when a participant is assigned D = d and Z = z and D ( Z = z ) to denote potential outcome of D when a participant is assigned Z = z . We write Y ( Z = z ) as a shorthand of Y ( D ( Z = z ) , Z = z ). Following Angrist et al. (1996), we say a participant is a complier under the IV dose pair { 0 g , 1 g } if { D ( Z = 0 g ) , D ( Z = 1 g ) } = (0 , 1), an always-taker if { D ( Z = 0 g ) , D ( Z = 1 g ) } = (1 , 1), a never-taker if { D ( Z = 0 g ) , D ( Z = 1 g ) } = (0 , 0), or a defier if { D ( Z = 0 g ) , D ( Z = 1 g ) } = (1 , 0), g ∈ { a, b } . We first describe identification assumptions.
texts.text: We will refer to the tuple (0 a , 1 a ) (or (0 b , 1 b )) as an IV dose pair (Sun and W¨ uthrich, 2022). We use Y ( D = d, Z = z ) to denote the potential outcome of Y when a participant is assigned D = d and Z = z and D ( Z = z ) to denote potential outcome of D when a participant is assigned Z = z . We write Y ( Z = z ) as a shorthand of Y ( D ( Z = z ) , Z = z ). Following Angrist et al. (1996), we say a participant is a complier under the IV dose pair { 0 g , 1 g } if { D ( Z = 0 g ) , D ( Z = 1 g ) } = (0 , 1), an always-taker if { D ( Z = 0 g ) , D ( Z = 1 g ) } = (1 , 1), a never-taker if { D ( Z = 0 g ) , D ( Z = 1 g ) } = (0 , 0), or a defier if { D ( Z = 0 g ) , D ( Z = 1 g ) } = (1 , 0), g ∈ { a, b } . We first describe identification assumptions.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Assumption 1. (Standard IV assumptions).
texts.text: Assumption 1. (Standard IV assumptions).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ( i ). (Consistency). D = D ( Z = z ) if Z = z , Y = Y ( D = d, Z = z ) if D = d and Z = z , for d ∈ { 0 , 1 } and z ∈ { 0 a , 1 a , 0 b , 1 b } .
texts.text: ( i ). (Consistency). D = D ( Z = z ) if Z = z , Y = Y ( D = d, Z = z ) if D = d and Z = z , for d ∈ { 0 , 1 } and z ∈ { 0 a , 1 a , 0 b , 1 b } .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ( ii ). (Exclusion restriction) . Y ( D = d, Z = z ) = Y ( D = d, Z = z ′ ) , for d ∈ { 0 , 1 } and z, z ′ ∈ { 0 a , 1 a , 0 b , 1 b } .
texts.text: ( ii ). (Exclusion restriction) . Y ( D = d, Z = z ) = Y ( D = d, Z = z ′ ) , for d ∈ { 0 , 1 } and z, z ′ ∈ { 0 a , 1 a , 0 b , 1 b } .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ( iii ). (IV independence). Z ⊥ ⊥ { D ( Z = z ) , Y ( D = d ) } | X = X , G = g , for all d , z and g .
texts.text: ( iii ). (IV independence). Z ⊥ ⊥ { D ( Z = z ) , Y ( D = d ) } | X = X , G = g , for all d , z and g .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ( iv ). (IV relevance). E P 0 [ D | Z = 1 g , X ] -E P 0 [ D | Z = 0 g , X ] = 0 and P 0 ( Z = z | X ) > 0 for g ∈ { a, b } , z ∈ { 0 a , 1 a , 0 b , 1 b } .
texts.text: ( iv ). (IV relevance). E P 0 [ D | Z = 1 g , X ] -E P 0 [ D | Z = 0 g , X ] = 0 and P 0 ( Z = z | X ) > 0 for g ∈ { a, b } , z ∈ { 0 a , 1 a , 0 b , 1 b } .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All four parts of Assumption 1 are standard IV identification assumptions and sometimes referred to as core IV assumptions (Angrist et al., 1996; Baiocchi et al., 2014). In a clinical trial like the PLCO study, the treatment assignment is most likely affecting clinical outcomes only via its effect on the treatment received, so exclusion restriction (Assumption 1(ii)) is often satisfied. We will use notation Y ( D = d ) as a shorthand of Y ( D = d, Z = z ) since the latter does not depend on z by exclusion restriction. Moreover, IV independence (Assumption 1(iii)) is automatically satisfied by design in a randomized trial, and IV relevance (Assumption 1(iv)) is verifiable and holds in most clinical trials.
texts.text: All four parts of Assumption 1 are standard IV identification assumptions and sometimes referred to as core IV assumptions (Angrist et al., 1996; Baiocchi et al., 2014). In a clinical trial like the PLCO study, the treatment assignment is most likely affecting clinical outcomes only via its effect on the treatment received, so exclusion restriction (Assumption 1(ii)) is often satisfied. We will use notation Y ( D = d ) as a shorthand of Y ( D = d, Z = z ) since the latter does not depend on z by exclusion restriction. Moreover, IV independence (Assumption 1(iii)) is automatically satisfied by design in a randomized trial, and IV relevance (Assumption 1(iv)) is verifiable and holds in most clinical trials.

texts.orig: In the nested IV framework, the IV dose pair that a participant receives depends on the stratification variable G : a participant will be randomly assigned to 0 a or 1 a if G = a , or 0 b or 1 b if G = b . In addition to the core IV assumptions, we consider the following set of nested IV assumptions :
texts.text: In the nested IV framework, the IV dose pair that a participant receives depends on the stratification variable G : a participant will be randomly assigned to 0 a or 1 a if G = a , or 0 b or 1 b if G = b . In addition to the core IV assumptions, we consider the following set of nested IV assumptions :

texts.orig: Assumption 2. (Nested IV assumptions).
texts.text: Assumption 2. (Nested IV assumptions).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ( i ). (Mean exchangeability over G ). E P 0 [ Y ( Z = 1 g ) -Y ( Z = 0 g ) | G = g, X ] = E P 0 [ Y ( Z = 1 g ) -Y ( Z = 0 g ) | X ] and E P 0 [ D ( Z = 1 g ) -D ( Z = 0 g ) | G = g, X ] = E P 0 [ D ( Z = 1 g ) -D ( Z = 0 g ) | X ] , g ∈ { a, b } .
texts.text: ( i ). (Mean exchangeability over G ). E P 0 [ Y ( Z = 1 g ) -Y ( Z = 0 g ) | G = g, X ] = E P 0 [ Y ( Z = 1 g ) -Y ( Z = 0 g ) | X ] and E P 0 [ D ( Z = 1 g ) -D ( Z = 0 g ) | G = g, X ] = E P 0 [ D ( Z = 1 g ) -D ( Z = 0 g ) | X ] , g ∈ { a, b } .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ( ii ). (Partial monotonicity). D ( Z = 1 g ) ≥ D ( Z = 0 g ) , g ∈ { a, b } .
texts.text: ( ii ). (Partial monotonicity). D ( Z = 1 g ) ≥ D ( Z = 0 g ) , g ∈ { a, b } .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ( iii ). (Non-equal compliance).
texts.text: ( iii ). (Non-equal compliance).

texts.orig: E P 0 [ D | Z = 1 a , X = x ] -E P 0 [ D | Z = 0 a , X = x ] = E P 0 [ D | Z = 1 b , X = x ] -E P 0 [ D | Z = 0 b , X = x ] .

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ( iv ). (Nested IV). { D ( Z = 0 a ) , D ( Z = 1 a ) } = (0 , 1) implies { D ( Z = 0 b ) , D ( Z = 1 b ) } = (0 , 1) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Assumption 2(i) states that, conditional on the observed covariates X , the average effect of IV Z (either version) on D and that on Y can be identified based on the corresponding stratum G = a or b . This assumption is satisfied if the stratification variable G is exogenous. For instance, in the PLCO study, the sudden shift from dual to single consent could be thought of as a happenstance for participants who enrolled right before and after this policy shift, analogous to the so-called 'regression discontinuity design' (Thistlethwaite and Campbell, 1960). This assumption is also satisfied, for example, if participating in the trial does not itself modify the effect of the IV on D (the treatment) or Y (the outcome), which is a standard assumption in the literature on generalizability (Dahabreh et al., 2019). Assumption 2(ii) states that there is no defier with respect to either version of the IV. Importantly, we do not impose any ordering among { 0 a , 0 b } , { 0 a , 1 b } , { 1 a , 0 b } , or { 1 a , 1 b } ; hence, this partial monotonicity assumption is weaker than the usual monotonicity assumption for an ordinal IV (Imbens and Angrist, 1994). Assumption 2(iii) states that the compliance rates corresponding to G = a and G = b are different; this is likely to hold when the IV is of different versions in two strata. Finally, Assumption 2(iv) states that if a participant is a complier under the IV pair { 0 a , 1 a } , then the participant is also a complier under the IV pair { 0 b , 1 b } . This assumption is key to our identification results and most likely to be satisfied when the binary IV Z ∈ { 0 b , 1 b } is stronger than the binary IV Z ∈ { 0 a , 1 a } . Empirically, the compliance rates can be calculated for

texts.orig: Remark 1 (One-sided non-compliance) . In clinical trials assessing an investigational product, participants assigned placebo often do not have access to the treatment. This is referred to as 'onesided non-compliance.' In this stylistic case, we necessarily have D ( Z = 0 a ) = D ( Z = 0 b ) = 0 , and the latent groups NT-AT, AT-NT and AAT in Table 1 no longer exist. Moreover, the switcher subgroup now only comprises participants who are never-takers under (0 a , 1 a ) but become compliers under (0 b , 1 b ) .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.5 Practical relevance of ACOATE and SWATE
texts.text: 2.5 Practical relevance of ACOATE and SWATE

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Causal estimands ACOATE and SWATE are examples of the so-called principal treatment effects (Frangakis and Rubin, 2002). As discussed in Section 1.1, the practical relevance of any principal stratum effects is always under close scrutiny and often with good reasons (Pearl, 2011; ? ). Responding to Pearl's (2011) critiques, in our opinion, SWATE and ACOATE are both a useful 'tool' in a holistic IV analysis and potentially interesting causal parameters in themselves. First, it is a 'tool' towards establishing the generalizability of a causal effect. For instance, researchers can immediately get a crude assessment of the generalizability of the treatment effect by comparing ACOATE to SWATE conditional on X . Such a testing strategy could be more powerful than

texts.orig: Based on Proposition 2, the mean and variance of each observed covariate, and more generally certain combinations of observed covariates (e.g., a projected risk score), in the always-complier and switcher subgroups can be characterized and compared to those in the entire study population. For instance, for a binary covariate X , a 'prevalence ratio' (Brookhart and Schneeweiss, 2007) may be calculated as P 0 ( X = 1 | S = ACO) /P 0 ( X = 1) and P 0 ( X = 1 | S = SW) /P 0 ( X = 1).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1 Vector IV representation and connection to vector IV literature
texts.text: 3.1 Vector IV representation and connection to vector IV literature

texts.label: text
texts.orig: So far, we have formulated Z ∈ { 0 a , 1 a , 0 b , 1 b } as a categorical IV. An alternative approach is to define a vector-valued IV, denoted as ( Z ∗ , G ), where Z ∗ ∈ { 0 , 1 } indicates whether an individual is assigned to the treatment group or the control group, and G is the stratification variable, which takes values in { a, b } . For G = g ∈ { a, b } , one may alternatively write D ( Z = 1 g ) as D ( Z ∗ = 1 , G = g ) and D ( Z = 0 g ) as D ( Z ∗ = 0 , G = g ). Under this vector IV representation, we may compare our partial monotonicity assumption (denoted as PM) to (1) the partial monotonicity assumption in
texts.text: So far, we have formulated Z ∈ { 0 a , 1 a , 0 b , 1 b } as a categorical IV. An alternative approach is to define a vector-valued IV, denoted as ( Z ∗ , G ), where Z ∗ ∈ { 0 , 1 } indicates whether an individual is assigned to the treatment group or the control group, and G is the stratification variable, which takes values in { a, b } . For G = g ∈ { a, b } , one may alternatively write D ( Z = 1 g ) as D ( Z ∗ = 1 , G = g ) and D ( Z = 0 g ) as D ( Z ∗ = 0 , G = g ). Under this vector IV representation, we may compare our partial monotonicity assumption (denoted as PM) to (1) the partial monotonicity assumption in

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mogstad et al. (2021) (denoted as PM2); (2) the limited monotonicity assumption in Hoff et al. (2024) (denoted as LiM); and (3) the vector monotonicity assumption in Goff (2024) (denoted as VM), when both Z ∗ and G are binary. In the Supplemental Material S1.1, we review these identification assumptions and discuss their relationship in detail. We show that PM2 and VM are equivalent in our setting, and PM, PM2 (VM) and LiM imply different principal strata. Moreover, the collection of 7 principal strata implied by the PM plus the nested IV assumption is neither a superset nor a subset of the principal strata implied by PM2 (VM) or LiM, highlighting the novelty of the identification assumptions developed in this article. In the Supplemental Material S1.2, we also discussed the difference between the SWATE and principal strata effects proposed in Goff (2024).
texts.text: Mogstad et al. (2021) (denoted as PM2); (2) the limited monotonicity assumption in Hoff et al. (2024) (denoted as LiM); and (3) the vector monotonicity assumption in Goff (2024) (denoted as VM), when both Z ∗ and G are binary. In the Supplemental Material S1.1, we review these identification assumptions and discuss their relationship in detail. We show that PM2 and VM are equivalent in our setting, and PM, PM2 (VM) and LiM imply different principal strata. Moreover, the collection of 7 principal strata implied by the PM plus the nested IV assumption is neither a superset nor a subset of the principal strata implied by PM2 (VM) or LiM, highlighting the novelty of the identification assumptions developed in this article. In the Supplemental Material S1.2, we also discussed the difference between the SWATE and principal strata effects proposed in Goff (2024).

When the nested IV assumption fails, two additional principal strata, those with { D ( Z = 0 a ) = 0 , D ( Z = 1 a ) = 1 , D ( Z = 0 b ) = 0 , D ( Z = 1 b ) = 0 } or { D ( Z = 0 a ) = 0 , D ( Z = 1 a ) = 1 , D ( Z = 0 b ) = 1 , D ( Z = 1 b ) = 1 } , will come into existence. We refer to the union of these two principal strata as D e F iers to the N ested IV assumption ( DFN ).

We refer to the union of these two principal strata as D e F iers to the N ested IV assumption ( DFN ).

texts.orig: 3.3 Multiple IV pairs; ordinal IV; strengthening an IV
texts.text: 3.3 Multiple IV pairs; ordinal IV; strengthening an IV

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The nested IV framework can be extended to multiple IV pairs. Let Z ∈ Z denote an IV, where Z is the support of Z . Throughout this subsection, we assume consistency, exclusion restriction and IV independence assumptions hold for any z ∈ Z , so Z is a valid IV. Let G denote an index set, and 0 g and 1 g denote two distinct elements of Z , where g ∈ G is the index. The ordered pair (0 g , 1 g ) is referred to as an IV pair, denoted as Z g , if D (1 g ) ≥ D (0 g ). Definition 1 formally defines a nested IV relation.
texts.text: The nested IV framework can be extended to multiple IV pairs. Let Z ∈ Z denote an IV, where Z is the support of Z . Throughout this subsection, we assume consistency, exclusion restriction and IV independence assumptions hold for any z ∈ Z , so Z is a valid IV. Let G denote an index set, and 0 g and 1 g denote two distinct elements of Z , where g ∈ G is the index. The ordered pair (0 g , 1 g ) is referred to as an IV pair, denoted as Z g , if D (1 g ) ≥ D (0 g ). Definition 1 formally defines a nested IV relation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 1. (Nested IV relation). Let g, g ′ ∈ G . IV pair Z g is said to be nested within IV pair Z g ′ , denoted as Z g ⪯ Z g ′ , if { ( D (0 g ) , D (1 g )) = (0 , 1) } ⊂ { ( D (0 g ′ ) , D (1 g ′ )) = (0 , 1) } . We also define Z g ⪰ Z g ′ if Z g ′ ⪯ Z g , and Z g ∼ Z g ′ if both Z g ⪰ Z g ′ and Z g ⪯ Z g ′ hold.
texts.text: Definition 1. (Nested IV relation). Let g, g ′ ∈ G . IV pair Z g is said to be nested within IV pair Z g ′ , denoted as Z g ⪯ Z g ′ , if { ( D (0 g ) , D (1 g )) = (0 , 1) } ⊂ { ( D (0 g ′ ) , D (1 g ′ )) = (0 , 1) } . We also define Z g ⪰ Z g ′ if Z g ′ ⪯ Z g , and Z g ∼ Z g ′ if both Z g ⪰ Z g ′ and Z g ⪯ Z g ′ hold.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The nested IV relation is a binary relation defined on the set of all IV pairs F := {Z g , g ∈ G} . Proposition 4 states the properties of the nested IV relation, which follows directly from the Definition 1.
texts.text: The nested IV relation is a binary relation defined on the set of all IV pairs F := {Z g , g ∈ G} . Proposition 4 states the properties of the nested IV relation, which follows directly from the Definition 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proposition 4. The nested IV relation ⪯ , as defined in Definition 1, is reflexive, transitive, but not necessarily complete. The relation ' ∼ ' defines an equivalence relation on F .
texts.text: Proposition 4. The nested IV relation ⪯ , as defined in Definition 1, is reflexive, transitive, but not necessarily complete. The relation ' ∼ ' defines an equivalence relation on F .

texts.orig: Formalizing the nested IV assumption as a binary relation is useful conceptually when the stratification variable G takes on multiple values. Below, we give three examples.
texts.text: Formalizing the nested IV assumption as a binary relation is useful conceptually when the stratification variable G takes on multiple values. Below, we give three examples.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Example 1 (Multicentre clinical trials) . When the objective is to pool data across clinical sites with varying degrees of compliance to estimate the switcher treatment effect, then G represents different clinical sites. For instance, in the PLCO study, when the Henry Ford Health System adopted a dual consent process, two other sites, University of Colorado (UC) and University of Alabama at Birmingham (UAB), both adopted a single consent process and had a high compliance rate. As we will demonstrate in the case study (Section 7), one may do an integrated data analysis by defining the stratification variable G ∈ { a, b, c } , where G = a corresponds to Henry Ford, G = b UC, and G = c UAB. In this analysis, Z = { 0 a , 1 a , 0 b , 1 b , 0 c , 1 c } , and it is reasonable to assume the IV pair (0 a , 1 a ) is nested within both (0 b , 1 b ) , denoted (0 a , 1 a ) ⪯ (0 b , 1 b ) , and (0 c , 1 c ) , denoted (0 a , 1 a ) ⪯ (0 c , 1 c ) , without imposing any relation between (0 b , 1 b ) and (0 c , 1 c ) because the nested IV assumption may not hold for UC and UAB participants. When viewed as a partial order, both (0 b , 1 b ) and (0 c , 1 c ) are maximal elements.
texts.text: Example 1 (Multicentre clinical trials) . When the objective is to pool data across clinical sites with varying degrees of compliance to estimate the switcher treatment effect, then G represents different clinical sites. For instance, in the PLCO study, when the Henry Ford Health System adopted a dual consent process, two other sites, University of Colorado (UC) and University of Alabama at Birmingham (UAB), both adopted a single consent process and had a high compliance rate. As we will demonstrate in the case study (Section 7), one may do an integrated data analysis by defining the stratification variable G ∈ { a, b, c } , where G = a corresponds to Henry Ford, G = b UC, and G = c UAB. In this analysis, Z = { 0 a , 1 a , 0 b , 1 b , 0 c , 1 c } , and it is reasonable to assume the IV pair (0 a , 1 a ) is nested within both (0 b , 1 b ) , denoted (0 a , 1 a ) ⪯ (0 b , 1 b ) , and (0 c , 1 c ) , denoted (0 a , 1 a ) ⪯ (0 c , 1 c ) , without imposing any relation between (0 b , 1 b ) and (0 c , 1 c ) because the nested IV assumption may not hold for UC and UAB participants. When viewed as a partial order, both (0 b , 1 b ) and (0 c , 1 c ) are maximal elements.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Example 2 (Ordinal IV) . An ordinal IV can be understood as a collection of IV pairs, equipped with a nested IV relation. For instance, let Z ∈ { 0 , 1 , 2 } be an ordinal IV with three levels. Suppose D ( Z ) is binary, D ( z ) ≥ D ( z ′ ) if z ≥ z ′ , and the population consists of a mixture of those with ( D (0) , D (1) , D (2)) = (0 , 0 , 0) , (0 , 0 , 1) , (0 , 1 , 1) , and (1 , 1 , 1) . This three-level ordinal IV can be decomposed into the following three IV pairs: (0 , 1) , (1 , 2) , and (0 , 2) . Among the three IV pairs, we
texts.text: Example 2 (Ordinal IV) . An ordinal IV can be understood as a collection of IV pairs, equipped with a nested IV relation. For instance, let Z ∈ { 0 , 1 , 2 } be an ordinal IV with three levels. Suppose D ( Z ) is binary, D ( z ) ≥ D ( z ′ ) if z ≥ z ′ , and the population consists of a mixture of those with ( D (0) , D (1) , D (2)) = (0 , 0 , 0) , (0 , 0 , 1) , (0 , 1 , 1) , and (1 , 1 , 1) . This three-level ordinal IV can be decomposed into the following three IV pairs: (0 , 1) , (1 , 2) , and (0 , 2) . Among the three IV pairs, we

texts.label: text
texts.orig: have the following nested IV relations: (0 , 1) ⪯ (0 , 2) , (1 , 2) ⪯ (0 , 2) ; however, neither (0 , 1) ⪰ (1 , 2) nor (0 , 1) ⪯ (1 , 2) holds.
texts.text: have the following nested IV relations: (0 , 1) ⪯ (0 , 2) , (1 , 2) ⪯ (0 , 2) ; however, neither (0 , 1) ⪰ (1 , 2) nor (0 , 1) ⪯ (1 , 2) holds.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Example 3 (Strengthening an IV) . A nested IV relation helps understand how an ordinal or continuous IV could be strengthened in a matched cohort study (Baiocchi et al., 2010; ? ). Let Z ∈ [0 , 1] denote a continuous IV. Suppose the treatment uptake D ( z ) is determined by some latent continuous variable D ∗ ( z ) by D ( z ) = ✶ { D ∗ ( z ) ≥ 3 4 } . Suppose the population consists of (1) those with D ∗ ( z ) = z ; and (2) those with D ∗ ( z ) = 1 2 z + 1 2 . The set of IV pairs can then be defined as { ( a, b ) : 0 ≤ a ≤ b ≤ 1 } . One may easily verify, for instance, that (0 , 1 3 ) is nested in (0 , 2 3 ) , and ( 2 3 , 1) is nested in ( 1 3 , 1) . Consider four participants with the same observed covariates to be matched, with Z ∈ { 0 , 1 / 3 , 2 / 3 , 1 } . By pairing the participant with Z = 0 with Z = 2 / 3 , and Z = 1 / 3 with Z = 1 , one may construct a matched cohort with higher compliance rate, compared to pairing Z = 0 with Z = 1 / 3 and Z = 2 / 3 with Z = 1 , as advocated in Baiocchi et al. (2010).
texts.text: Example 3 (Strengthening an IV) . A nested IV relation helps understand how an ordinal or continuous IV could be strengthened in a matched cohort study (Baiocchi et al., 2010; ? ). Let Z ∈ [0 , 1] denote a continuous IV. Suppose the treatment uptake D ( z ) is determined by some latent continuous variable D ∗ ( z ) by D ( z ) = ✶ { D ∗ ( z ) ≥ 3 4 } . Suppose the population consists of (1) those with D ∗ ( z ) = z ; and (2) those with D ∗ ( z ) = 1 2 z + 1 2 . The set of IV pairs can then be defined as { ( a, b ) : 0 ≤ a ≤ b ≤ 1 } . One may easily verify, for instance, that (0 , 1 3 ) is nested in (0 , 2 3 ) , and ( 2 3 , 1) is nested in ( 1 3 , 1) . Consider four participants with the same observed covariates to be matched, with Z ∈ { 0 , 1 / 3 , 2 / 3 , 1 } . By pairing the participant with Z = 0 with Z = 2 / 3 , and Z = 1 / 3 with Z = 1 , one may construct a matched cohort with higher compliance rate, compared to pairing Z = 0 with Z = 1 / 3 and Z = 2 / 3 with Z = 1 , as advocated in Baiocchi et al. (2010).

texts.text: 4 Estimation and inference

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Estimation of the always-complier average treatment effect follows immediately by applying the estimation and inferential methods in the standard IV literature to the binary IV pair (0 a , 1 a ); see, e.g., Fr¨ olich (2007) and Kennedy (2022). In this section, we will focus on estimation and inference of the switcher average treatment effect.
texts.text: Estimation of the always-complier average treatment effect follows immediately by applying the estimation and inferential methods in the standard IV literature to the binary IV pair (0 a , 1 a ); see, e.g., Fr¨ olich (2007) and Kennedy (2022). In this section, we will focus on estimation and inference of the switcher average treatment effect.

texts.orig: 4.3 Non-parametric efficient estimators
texts.text: 4.3 Non-parametric efficient estimators

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The efficient influence function in Theorem 3 motivates an asymptotically efficient, one-step estimator and an estimating equation-based estimator. A one-step estimator is based on first order bias correction of a plug-in estimator (Pfanzagl, 1990); an estimating equation-based estimator is based on solving an efficient influence function-based estimating equation (van der Laan and Robins, 2003; Chernozhukov et al., 2018a). Here we will focus on the estimating equation-based estimator and leave the one-step estimator to Supplemental Material S2.7.
texts.text: The efficient influence function in Theorem 3 motivates an asymptotically efficient, one-step estimator and an estimating equation-based estimator. A one-step estimator is based on first order bias correction of a plug-in estimator (Pfanzagl, 1990); an estimating equation-based estimator is based on solving an efficient influence function-based estimating equation (van der Laan and Robins, 2003; Chernozhukov et al., 2018a). Here we will focus on the estimating equation-based estimator and leave the one-step estimator to Supplemental Material S2.7.

texts.text: Next, we propose formal statistical procedures testing the null hypothesis:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We note that if X is empty or low dimensional, then one might postulate parametric models for θ ACO ,P 0 ( X ) and θ SW ,P 0 ( X ), along with additional conditional mean models for Y and D . In that case, one can test e.g. θ ACO ,P 0 ( X ) -θ SW ,P 0 ( X ) = 0 using standard over-identification tests, such as the Sargan-Hansen test. However, when X is moderate in dimension, then such tests may be prone both to inflated type-I error (due to nuisance model misspecification) and lack of power (relative to tests that allow for a more flexible set of alternatives). Two flexible non-parametric testing procedures are therefore described below.
texts.text: We note that if X is empty or low dimensional, then one might postulate parametric models for θ ACO ,P 0 ( X ) and θ SW ,P 0 ( X ), along with additional conditional mean models for Y and D . In that case, one can test e.g. θ ACO ,P 0 ( X ) -θ SW ,P 0 ( X ) = 0 using standard over-identification tests, such as the Sargan-Hansen test. However, when X is moderate in dimension, then such tests may be prone both to inflated type-I error (due to nuisance model misspecification) and lack of power (relative to tests that allow for a more flexible set of alternatives). Two flexible non-parametric testing procedures are therefore described below.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Next, we describe a class of non-parametric L p norm-based tests motivated by Westling (2022). Again, we consider testing a null hypothesis H 0 : ξ P 0 = 0 a.s. P 0 versus H 1 : ξ P 0 = 0, where ξ P 0 ∈ L 2 ( P 0 ) is a function of X . Suppose X = ( X 1 , X 2 ), where the marginal distribution of X 1 is absolutely continuous to the counting measure, and the marginal distribution of X 2 is absolutely continuous to the Lebesgue measure. Let ∥ ξ P 0 ∥ P 0 ,p = ( ∫ | ξ P 0 | p dP 0 ) 1 /p for p ≥ 1, and ∥ ξ P 0 ∥ P 0 , ∞ = sup x ∈X ξ ( x ). Let the support of X = ( X 1 , X 2 ) be X = X 1 × X 2 ⊂ R d 1 × R d 2 . Let 1 c ( x ) = 1 { x ∈ ( -∞ , c 1 ] × ... × ( -∞ , c d 1 ] × ... × ( -∞ , c d 1 + d 2 ] } . The construction of the L p norm-based tests is based on the following technical lemma.
texts.text: Next, we describe a class of non-parametric L p norm-based tests motivated by Westling (2022). Again, we consider testing a null hypothesis H 0 : ξ P 0 = 0 a.s. P 0 versus H 1 : ξ P 0 = 0, where ξ P 0 ∈ L 2 ( P 0 ) is a function of X . Suppose X = ( X 1 , X 2 ), where the marginal distribution of X 1 is absolutely continuous to the counting measure, and the marginal distribution of X 2 is absolutely continuous to the Lebesgue measure. Let ∥ ξ P 0 ∥ P 0 ,p = ( ∫ | ξ P 0 | p dP 0 ) 1 /p for p ≥ 1, and ∥ ξ P 0 ∥ P 0 , ∞ = sup x ∈X ξ ( x ). Let the support of X = ( X 1 , X 2 ) be X = X 1 × X 2 ⊂ R d 1 × R d 2 . texts.text: Lemma 1. The following statements are equivalent: (i). θ P 0 ( x ) = 0 for all x ∈ X 1 × X 2 . (ii). Ω P 0 ( c ) := ∫ ξ P 0 ( x )1 c ( x ) dP 0 ( x ) = 0 for all c ∈ X . (iii). ∥ Ω P 0 ∥ P 0 ,p = 0 for p ≥ 1 or p = ∞ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Therefore, testing the null hypothesis ξ P 0 ( x ) = 0 a.s. P 0 is equivalent to testing the null hypothesis that Ω P 0 ( c ) = 0 for any c ∈ X . One advantage of constructing a test based on Ω P 0 ( c ) is that Ω P 0 ( c ) is √ n -estimable for any fixed c without further parametric assumptions. On the contrary, even when all the nuisance parameters and potential outcomes are known, θ P 0 ( x ) is not generally √ n -estimable.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 7 (Computation cost) . Unlike T ( j ) Wald , the proposed non-parametric L p norm-based tests are computationally challenging when the dimension of X is moderately large, because the procedure requires simulating a Gaussian process indexed by a subset of R d x . On the other hand, the computation cost of the best least squares projection-based test is essentially agnostic to the dimension of X .
texts.text: Remark 7 (Computation cost) . Unlike T ( j ) Wald , the proposed non-parametric L p norm-based tests are computationally challenging when the dimension of X is moderately large, because the procedure requires simulating a Gaussian process indexed by a subset of R d x . On the other hand, the computation cost of the best least squares projection-based test is essentially agnostic to the dimension of X .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6 Simulation study
texts.text: 6 Simulation study

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have three goals in this simulation study. First, we investigate the finite-sample performance of the proposed estimators for SWATE. Second, we investigate the level of each projection-based test for the null hypotheses H ( j ) 0 . Because test T ( j ) Wald ,α developed for H ( j ) 0 , j = 1 , 2 , 3, can each be used to test the same null hypothesis Assumption H(i) or H(ii), we also compare their power. Third, we compare the level and power of the projection-based test and two non-parametric L p norm-based tests.
texts.text: We have three goals in this simulation study. First, we investigate the finite-sample performance of the proposed estimators for SWATE. Second, we investigate the level of each projection-based test for the null hypotheses H ( j ) 0 . Because test T ( j ) Wald ,α developed for H ( j ) 0 , j = 1 , 2 , 3, can each be used to test the same null hypothesis Assumption H(i) or H(ii), we also compare their power. Third, we compare the level and power of the projection-based test and two non-parametric L p norm-based tests.

For each simulated dataset, we constructed the proposed estimator ̂ ψ ee with all nuisance functions estimated using the R (R Core Team, 2013) package SuperLearner (Van der Laan et al., 2007). Specifically, we included the random forest and generalized linear models in the SuperLearner library. For each of the 2 × 4 × 3 × 2 = 48 settings determined by the outcome type, sample size, ( α 1 , α 2 , α 3 ) and ( β 1 , β 2 , β 3 ), we repeated the simulation 1000 times. The standard error of ̂ ψ ee was estimated based on the efficient influence function, as described in Section 4.3.

texts.orig: Figure S.1 plots the sampling distributions of estimating equation-based estimators for various
texts.text: Figure S.1 plots the sampling distributions of estimating equation-based estimators for various

texts.label: text
texts.orig: combinations of sample size and switcher proportion when the outcome is continuous and β = (2 , 2 , 2). The true values of SWATE are superimposed as red dash lines. Overall, the sampling distributions appeared normal except in settings where both the proportion of switchers and the sample size are small (e.g., n = 1000 with 11% switchers). For a fixed proportion of switchers, the sampling distributions are more concentrated around the true values as the sample size increases. Similarly, for a fixed sample size, the sampling distribution becomes more concentrated around the true values as the proportion of switchers increases. Table 2 further reports the bias, relative bias and coverage probability for different settings. We have observed come consistent trends. First, when the proportion of switchers is small, say 11%, then a large sample size (e.g., n = 10000) is needed for the proposed estimator to achieve a small relative bias and its 95% confidence intervals to achieve nominal coverage. This phenomenon is known as the weak IV bias (see, e.g., Bound et al., 1995; Andrews et al., 2019). On the other hand, when the proportion of switchers is relatively high (e.g., 66%), then the relative bias is small and the coverage attains approximately its nominal level for a moderately large sample size. For instance, when β = (2 , 2 , 2), the relative bias of the proposed estimator is 2 . 1% and the coverage of the 95% CI is 94 . 1% when the sample size is as small as 1000. Table 2 also reports the proportion of times the estimator becomes unstable. This only happened when both the sample size and proportion of switchers were small. For instance, when n = 2000 and the proportion of switchers is 32%, none of the 1000 simulated datasets returned an unstable estimator. On the other hand, the one-step estimator was a bit more unstable compared to the estimating equation-based estimator; see Table S3 in Supplemental Material S3.

Table 2 also reports the proportion of times the estimator becomes unstable. This only happened when both the sample size and proportion of switchers were small. For instance, when n = 2000 and the proportion of switchers is 32%, none of the 1000 simulated datasets returned an unstable estimator. On the other hand, the one-step estimator was a bit more unstable compared to the estimating equation-based estimator; see Table S3 in Supplemental Material S3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Overall, our simulation studies suggest that when the sample size times the proportion of switchers is greater than 500, the performance of our proposed estimator is favorable with small relative bias and close-to-nominal coverage. Simulation results for a binary outcome are qualitatively similar; see Supplemental Material S3 for details.

texts.orig: Figure S.5 in the Supplemental Material S3 plots the nominal Type-I error rate versus the empirical rejection proportion for the projection-based test. For T (1) Wald ,α that compares SWATE P 0 ( X ) to ACOATE P 0 ( X ), the rejection fraction aligns well with the nominal level when the proportion of switchers is not too high or too low. On the other hand, the approximation of the null distribution is not accurate when the proportion of switchers is small and hence SWATE P 0 ( X ) is not accurately estimated, or when the proportion of always-compliers is small and hence ACOATE P 0 ( X ) is not well estimated. Analogously, the null distribution of T (2) Wald ,α is best approximated when the proportion of switchers is small and that of T (3) Wald ,α is best approximated when the proportion of switchers is large. Importantly, we did not observe an inflation of type-I error for a level-0 . 05 test in any setting. We observed similar patterns for the Type-I error control for the L 2 and L ∞ norm-based tests (Figure S.6 and S.8 in the Supplemental Material S4).

texts.orig: Figure 1 further compares the power of each test under different settings. In all settings, when the proportion of switchers is small (e.g., 9% and 18%), T (2) Wald , 0 . 05 often outperforms T (1) Wald , 0 . 05 and T (3) Wald , 0 . 05 , and T (3) Wald , 0 . 05 is the least powerful. When the proportion of switchers is high (e.g., 72% and 81%), T (3) Wald , 0 . 05 is the most powerful among three tests. Lastly, when the proportion of switchers is moderate (e.g., 36% and 54%), T (2) Wald , 0 . 05 outperforms the other two tests although the three tests have similar performance when the sample size is large. As the sample size increases, the power of all three tests increases. In empirical studies, we would recommend T (2) Wald , 0 . 05 when the proportion of always-compliers is high and T (3) Wald , 0 . 05 when the proportion of switchers is high.

texts.orig: Finally, we compared the computation cost of the projection-based and L p norm-based, nonparametric tests. When n = 5000, it took roughly one minute to conduct a projection-based test, with cross-fitting using machine learning methods taking the most computation time. On the other hand, it took more than 15 minutes to conduct a L p norm-based, non-parametric test, as simulating a Gaussian process could be computationally intensive.

texts.text: 7 Revisiting the PLCO study

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We applied our developed nested IV framework to re-analyzing the Prostate, Lung, Colorectal and Ovarian (PLCO) Cancer Screening Trial. We first focused on the Henry Ford Health System, which shifted from dual to single consent in the middle of 1997. Our analysis cohort consisted of participants who enrolled prior to 1997 ( G = a ) and after 1997 ( G = b ). We excluded those enrolled during 1997. Table 3 summarizes important baseline covariates and treatment uptake (attending first scheduled cancer screening or not) by stage and treatment assignment. Participants enrolled in the two stages differed moderately in their baseline covariates; within each stage, participants were comparable in the treatment and control arms due to randomization.

texts.orig: Finally, we tested the null hypothesis that the effect of cancer screening on colorectal cancer rate was the same for all principal strata, within strata defined by 6 observed covariates including age, sex, race, education, smoking status, and BMI. The test statistic equaled 7 . 31, 9 . 29 and 7 . 75 for three best-projection-based tests T (1) Wald , 0 . 05 , T (2) Wald , 0 . 05 , and T (3) Wald , 0 . 05 , respectively. The 95% quantile of a χ 2 (9) distribution is 16 . 92; hence, results from all these tests did not provide evidence supporting further effect heterogeneity among latent principal strata within strata defined by 6 observed covariates.

We also found little evidence supporting additional effect heterogeneity within latent principal strata within strata defined by observed covariates.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 8 Discussion
texts.text: 8 Discussion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, we systematically study the problem with a pair of nested instrumental variables. We show that the switcher average treatment effect, or SWATE, can be non-parametrically identified under a novel nested IV assumption and develop an efficient-influence-function-based estimator for it. We also develop a projection-based test and a non-parametric test that formally assess the effect-homogeneity-type assumptions under our proposed nested IV framework.
texts.text: In this work, we systematically study the problem with a pair of nested instrumental variables. We show that the switcher average treatment effect, or SWATE, can be non-parametrically identified under a novel nested IV assumption and develop an efficient-influence-function-based estimator for it. We also develop a projection-based test and a non-parametric test that formally assess the effect-homogeneity-type assumptions under our proposed nested IV framework.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our paper contributes to the discussion on violation of the SUTVA (Imbens and Rubin, 2015). In the PLCO trial and similar multicentre or multiphasic trials, there may exist multiple versions of encouragement for treatment uptake, a subtlety that could be easily overlooked. In the presence of multiple versions of an IV, the usual intention-to-treat effect and the Wald estimand do not have their usual causal interpretations. The differentiation between two distinct versions of IV gives us an opportunity to study additional principal causal effects induced by the nested IV structure and test for effect heterogeneity. Interestingly, our identified conditional switcher average treatment has a similar form as the conditional ATE under the so-called instrumented difference-in-differences setting (Ye et al., 2023); however, the SWATE estimand proposed in this article is obtained by integrating over the covariate distribution among switchers, while Ye et al.'s (2023) marginalized estimand is obtained by integrating over the covariate distribution of the entire study population. Furthermore, in our setting, the abundance of principal strata complicates the model for treatment uptake and potential outcomes; hence, the current article focused on non-parametric estimators that permit using flexible non-parametric methods for nuisance functions estimation rather the usual doubly robust estimators that require correct specification for parametric nuisance models (Rotnitzky et al., 2020).
texts.text: Our paper contributes to the discussion on violation of the SUTVA (Imbens and Rubin, 2015). The differentiation between two distinct versions of IV gives us an opportunity to study additional principal causal effects induced by the nested IV structure and test for effect heterogeneity. Interestingly, our identified conditional switcher average treatment has a similar form as the conditional ATE under the so-called instrumented difference-in-differences setting (Ye et al., 2023); however, the SWATE estimand proposed in this article is obtained by integrating over the covariate distribution among switchers, while Ye et al.'s (2023) marginalized estimand is obtained by integrating over the covariate distribution of the entire study population. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our methods also provide some insight into testing the equality of two function-valued parameters (Hudson et al., 2021, 2023). We provide a general framework for such testing problems by projecting the function-valued parameter to a subspace of L 2 ( P ). In principle, one can project the parameters onto an infinite-dimensional space; in practice, projection onto a finite dimensional space suffices for type-I error control and appears to have good finite-sample power. Moreover, compared to a non-parametric test, e.g., our generalization of Westling (2022), a projection-based test is computationally more efficient and easier to implement when covariates are of moderate to high dimension.
texts.text: Our methods also provide some insight into testing the equality of two function-valued parameters (Hudson et al., 2021, 2023). We provide a general framework for such testing problems by projecting the function-valued parameter to a subspace of L 2 ( P ). In principle, one can project the parameters onto an infinite-dimensional space; in practice, projection onto a finite dimensional space suffices for type-I error control and appears to have good finite-sample power. Moreover, compared to a non-parametric test, e.g., our generalization of Westling (2022), a projection-based test is computationally more efficient and easier to implement when covariates are of moderate to high dimension.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Switcher average treatment effect is an example of the principal treatment effect. Principal effects are sometimes criticized for not being the most relevant for clinical decision making as they are concerned about the effects among unidentified subgroups; however, SWATE is clinically meaningful when researchers are interested in the practical utility of improving the compliance: a small SWATE effectively conveys the message that the marginal gain of improving compliance may be low for a target population, while a large SWATE could be an encouraging marching order for implementation scientists to investigate how to improve treatment adherence which has potential to translate to improved clinical outcomes.
texts.text: Switcher average treatment effect is an example of the principal treatment effect. Principal effects are sometimes criticized for not being the most relevant for clinical decision making as they are concerned about the effects among unidentified subgroups; however, SWATE is clinically meaningful when researchers are interested in the practical utility of improving the compliance: a small SWATE effectively conveys the message that the marginal gain of improving compliance may be low for a target population, while a large SWATE could be an encouraging marching order for implementation scientists to investigate how to improve treatment adherence which has potential to translate to improved clinical outcomes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The assumption of mean exchangeability over G , which may represent time (as in the analysis of the policy shift in Henry Ford Health System in Section 7) or different clinical sites (as in the integrated analysis of multicentre trial data in Supplemental Material S6.1), is crucial. In the case of a sudden policy shift, an unconditional version of mean exchangeability may be sufficient, as G is likely to be exogenous. However, in the integrated clinical trial analysis, differences in trial participation between the University of Colorado and Henry Ford Health System could influence the effect of the IV on treatment uptake, or even the effect of treatment uptake on clinical outcomes. Therefore, conditioning on observed covariates is necessary to reduce the risk of residual confounding.
texts.text: The assumption of mean exchangeability over G , which may represent time (as in the analysis of the policy shift in Henry Ford Health System in Section 7) or different clinical sites (as in the integrated analysis of multicentre trial data in Supplemental Material S6.1), is crucial. In the case of a sudden policy shift, an unconditional version of mean exchangeability may be sufficient, as G is likely to be exogenous. However, in the integrated clinical trial analysis, differences in trial participation between the University of Colorado and Henry Ford Health System could influence the effect of the IV on treatment uptake, or even the effect of treatment uptake on clinical outcomes. Therefore, conditioning on observed covariates is necessary to reduce the risk of residual confounding.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In general, even a valid IV cannot point identify the marginal and conditional treatment effects without restricting heterogeneity in some sense. Since it was an aim of this work to investigate treatment effect heterogeneity with non-compliance, we did not impose these type of restrictions. It is an interesting question for future work whether alternative non-principal interpretations of the ACOATE and SWATE functionals exist, without imposing homogeneity. In this article, we focus
