texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: S1.1 Monotonicity assumptions
texts.text: S1.1 Monotonicity assumptions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An alternative approach for defining the potential outcomes is by using a vector-valued instrumental variable ( Z ∗ , G ), where Z ∗ ∈ { 0 , 1 } indicates whether an individual is assigned to the treatment group or the control group, and G is the stratification variable, taking values in { a, b } . For g ∈ { a, b } , one may alternatively write D ( Z = 1 g ) as D ( Z ∗ = 1 , G = g ) and D ( Z = 0 g ) as D ( Z ∗ = 0 , G = g ). Under this vector-valued IV formulation, we may compare our partial monotonicity assumption (denoted as PM) to (1) the partial monotonicity assumption in Mogstad et al. (2021) (denoted as PM2); (2) the limited monotonicity assumption in Hoff et al. (2024) (denoted as LiM); and (3) the vector monotonicity assumption in Goff (2024) (denoted as VM), when both Z ∗ and G are binary.
texts.text: An alternative approach for defining the potential outcomes is by using a vector-valued instrumental variable ( Z ∗ , G ), where Z ∗ ∈ { 0 , 1 } indicates whether an individual is assigned to the treatment group or the control group, and G is the stratification variable, taking values in { a, b } . For g ∈ { a, b } , one may alternatively write D ( Z = 1 g ) as D ( Z ∗ = 1 , G = g ) and D ( Z = 0 g ) as D ( Z ∗ = 0 , G = g ). Under this vector-valued IV formulation, we may compare our partial monotonicity assumption (denoted as PM) to (1) the partial monotonicity assumption in Mogstad et al. (2021) (denoted as PM2); (2) the limited monotonicity assumption in Hoff et al. (2024) (denoted as LiM); and (3) the vector monotonicity assumption in Goff (2024) (denoted as VM), when both Z ∗ and G are binary.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Below, we first rewrite each monotonicity assumption using the notation in the current manuscript:
texts.text: Below, we first rewrite each monotonicity assumption using the notation in the current manuscript:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Definition S1. 1. (PM). D ( Z ∗ = 1 , G = g ) ≥ D ( Z ∗ = 0 , G = g ) for g ∈ { a, b } .
texts.text: Definition S1. 1. (PM). D ( Z ∗ = 1 , G = g ) ≥ D ( Z ∗ = 0 , G = g ) for g ∈ { a, b } .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. (PM2). D ( Z ∗ = 1 , G = g ) ≥ D ( Z ∗ = 0 , G = g ) for g ∈ { a, b } and D ( Z ∗ = z, G = a ) ≥ D ( Z ∗ = z, G = b ) for z ∈ { 0 , 1 } .
texts.text: (PM2). D ( Z ∗ = 1 , G = g ) ≥ D ( Z ∗ = 0 , G = g ) for g ∈ { a, b } and D ( Z ∗ = z, G = a ) ≥ D ( Z ∗ = z, G = b ) for z ∈ { 0 , 1 } .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. (VM). D ( Z ∗ = 1 , G = g ) ≥ D ( Z ∗ = 0 , G = g ) or and D ( Z ∗ = z, G = a ) ≥ D ( Z ∗ = z, G = b ) for z ∈ { 0 , 1 } .
texts.text: (VM). D ( Z ∗ = 1 , G = g ) ≥ D ( Z ∗ = 0 , G = g ) or and D ( Z ∗ = z, G = a ) ≥ D ( Z ∗ = z, G = b ) for z ∈ { 0 , 1 } .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the definition of PM2 , we imposed similar direction constraint as equation (7) of Mogstad et al. (2021). Under the setting considered in this article, we have the following relationship among these 4 definitions:
texts.text: For the definition of PM2 , we imposed similar direction constraint as equation (7) of Mogstad et al. (2021). Under the setting considered in this article, we have the following relationship among these 4 definitions:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. PM2 and VM are equivalent.
texts.text: PM2 and VM are equivalent.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. PM2 implies PM and LiM .
texts.text: PM2 implies PM and LiM .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. PM and LiM don't imply each other.
texts.text: 3. PM and LiM don't imply each other.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Importantly, PM, PM2 (VM) and LiM imply the existence of different principal strata, which is summarized in Table S1. LiM is the weakest assumption and implies 12 out of 16 possible principal strata. PM2 (VM) implies 6 principal strata, which is also a proper subset of the 9 principal strata PM implies. PM, together with the key nested IV assumption considered in the current article, implies 7 principal strata, including Always-Taker-Never-Taker (AT-NT) and Always-TakerComplier (a particular type of Switcher), that do not exist under PM2 (VM). Complier-AlwaysTaker, which exists under PM2 (VM), no longer exists under PM + Nested IV.
texts.text: Importantly, PM, PM2 (VM) and LiM imply the existence of different principal strata, which is summarized in Table S1. LiM is the weakest assumption and implies 12 out of 16 possible principal strata. PM2 (VM) implies 6 principal strata, which is also a proper subset of the 9 principal strata PM implies. PM, together with the key nested IV assumption considered in the current article, implies 7 principal strata, including Always-Taker-Never-Taker (AT-NT) and Always-TakerComplier (a particular type of Switcher), that do not exist under PM2 (VM). Complier-AlwaysTaker, which exists under PM2 (VM), no longer exists under PM + Nested IV.

texts.orig: S6.1 Integrated analysis of multiple clinical sites
texts.text: S6.1 Integrated analysis of multiple clinical sites

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We consider an integrated analysis of three clinical sites as a second application of the proposed method. We consider the Henry Ford Health system during the dual consent stage (denoted as G = a ), University of Colorado (denoted as G = b ), and University of Alabama at Birmingham (denoted as G = c ). The compliance rates in these three sites are 50.9%, 82.6% and 96.0%, respectively (see Table S5 in Supplementary Material). We consider the following nested IV assumption: IV pair Z a = { 1 a , 0 a } is nested in Z b = { 1 b , 0 b } , and Z c = { 1 c , 0 c } . Using the notation introduced in Section 3.3, we can write Z a ⪯ Z b and Z a ⪯ Z c . Two switcher populations can be defined accordingly, one associated with Z a and Z b and the other with Z a and Z c .
texts.text: We consider an integrated analysis of three clinical sites as a second application of the proposed method. We consider the Henry Ford Health system during the dual consent stage (denoted as G = a ), University of Colorado (denoted as G = b ), and University of Alabama at Birmingham (denoted as G = c ). The compliance rates in these three sites are 50.9%, 82.6% and 96.0%, respectively (see Table S5 in Supplementary Material). We consider the following nested IV assumption: IV pair Z a = { 1 a , 0 a } is nested in Z b = { 1 b , 0 b } , and Z c = { 1 c , 0 c } . Using the notation introduced in Section 3.3, we can write Z a ⪯ Z b and Z a ⪯ Z c . Two switcher populations can be defined accordingly, one associated with Z a and Z b and the other with Z a and Z c .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We also implemented the proposed one-step estimator and estimating equation-based estimator for the switcher average treatment effects. For the dataset with Henry Fold Health system and University of Colorado, the risk difference was estimated to be -0 . 008 (95% CI: -0 . 033 to 0 . 017) for switchers and -0 . 011 (95% CI: -0 . 023 to 0 . 001) for always-compliers based on the one-step estimator. The results obtained from the estimating equation-based estimator were almost the same: -0 . 008 (95% CI: -0 . 033 to 0 . 017) for switchers and -0 . 011 (95% CI: -0 . 023 to 0 . 001) for always-compliers. The risk difference estimated by Wald estimator is -0 . 012 (95%CI: -0 . 040 to 0 . 015) or switchers and -0 . 009 (95% CI: -0 . 021 to 0 . 002 for always-compliers). The test statistics based on best least-squares projection are 5.06, 3.31 and 5.92, respectively. Therefore there is no evidence to reject the null hypothesis that there is no treatment effect heterogeneity. For the dataset with Henry Fold Health system and University of Alabama Birmingham, the 15-year risk difference was estimated to be -0 . 006 (95% CI: -0 . 017 to 0 . 006) for switchers and -0 . 008 (95% CI: -0 . 017 to 0 . 002) for always-compliers based on the one-step estimator. The results obtained from the estimating equation-based estimator were also very similar: -0 . 006 (95% CI: -0 . 017 to 0 . 006) for switchers and -0 . 008 (95% CI: -0 . 017 to 0 . 002) for always-compliers. The risk difference estimated by Wald estimator is 0 . 002 (95%CI: -0 . 018 to 0 . 022) for switchers and -0 . 007 (95% CI: -0 . 018 to 0 . 004 for always-compliers). The test statistics based on best least-squares projection are 3.88, 4.56 and 4.11 respectively. Therefore, we have no evidence to reject the null hypothesis.
texts.text: We also implemented the proposed one-step estimator and estimating equation-based estimator for the switcher average treatment effects. For the dataset with Henry Fold Health system and University of Colorado, the risk difference was estimated to be -0 . 008 (95% CI: -0 . 033 to 0 . 017) for switchers and -0 . 011 (95% CI: -0 . 023 to 0 . 001) for always-compliers based on the one-step estimator. The results obtained from the estimating equation-based estimator were almost the same: -0 . 008 (95% CI: -0 . 033 to 0 . 017) for switchers and -0 . 011 (95% CI: -0 . 023 to 0 . 001) for always-compliers. The risk difference estimated by Wald estimator is -0 . 012 (95%CI: -0 . 040 to 0 . 015) or switchers and -0 . 009 (95% CI: -0 . 021 to 0 . 002 for always-compliers). The test statistics based on best least-squares projection are 5.06, 3.31 and 5.92, respectively. Therefore there is no evidence to reject the null hypothesis that there is no treatment effect heterogeneity. For the dataset with Henry Fold Health system and University of Alabama Birmingham, the 15-year risk difference was estimated to be -0 . 006 (95% CI: -0 . 017 to 0 . 006) for switchers and -0 . 008 (95% CI: -0 . 017 to 0 . 002) for always-compliers based on the one-step estimator. The results obtained from the estimating equation-based estimator were also very similar: -0 . 006 (95% CI: -0 . 017 to 0 . 006) for switchers and -0 . 008 (95% CI: -0 . 017 to 0 . 002) for always-compliers. The risk difference estimated by Wald estimator is 0 . 002 (95%CI: -0 . 018 to 0 . 022) for switchers and -0 . 007 (95% CI: -0 . 018 to 0 . 004 for always-compliers). The test statistics based on best least-squares projection are 3.88, 4.56 and 4.11 respectively. Therefore, we have no evidence to reject the null hypothesis.
